original_abstract: In Slovakia, the growing success of new parties with anti-corruption, anti-establishment, and populist appeals has disrupted the previous pattern of alternations of the blocks of right-wing and left-wing coalitions. However, the heterogeneity and participation of many new parties in government coalitions have not hindered cabinet formation and the establishment of coalition agreements. We can also observe a stabilization of the rules and functioning patterns of coalition government, which are in line with the Ministerial Government Model. Coalition governments are based on the existence of a public coalition agreement signed by the party leaders, which explicitly determines the allocation of offices, the management of coalition relations, and the settlement of disputes through the institution of the coalition council. This process of stabilization of the rules and mechanisms of the functioning of coalitions was not significantly affected by the events of the financial or migration crisis, which hit Slovakia only to a limited extent and are not even associated with changes in the quality of democracy. On the contrary, the pandemic provided an opportunity for the temporary dominance of the prime minister and thus disrupted a gradual establishment of more of a coalition governance model.
humanized_abstract: The new anti-corruption and populist parties have finally started to succeed in Slovakia. Thus, the alternating of right and left wing coalition blocks came to an end. However, coalition negotiation and coalition agreements have not been a problem given the high degree of fragmentation and the large number of parties participating in the coalitions. The pattern of the rules and practices within the coalitions have stabilized within the framework of the Ministerial Government Model. While the main characteristics such as the coalition negotiations involving political party leaders and the conclusion of a public programme agreement for the coalition indicating the distribution of cabinet offices or assigning responsibility for areas in need of policy solution are the same as those in the previous two electoral cycles, the pandemic was also an exception in relation to the governance model established in the Slovak coalition government, when the prime minister has temporarily taken the full spotlight of politics and it has thus been hindered to move further towards the direction of coalition-based type of governance.
